document_purpose.summary: RFC 9293 is the consolidated, updated IETF Standards Track base specification for TCP, replacing RFC 793 and six other RFCs (879, 2873, 6093, 6429, 6528, 6691). It integrates decades of errata, security improvements, and normative clarifications into a single implementation reference, updating RFC 1011, RFC 1122, and RFC 5961.
document_purpose.implementation_scope: Full normative TCP implementation: header format and options, connection lifecycle (open/close/reset), sequence number management, data transfer, flow control (window management, SWS avoidance, delayed ACKs, ZWP), segmentation (MSS, PMTUD, Nagle), retransmission and congestion control hooks, keep-alives, urgent data, user/TCP API, TCP/IP interface, and event-driven state machine across all 11 connection states.
non_goals: Design philosophy and rationale (delegated to RFC 793 and TCP Roadmap RFC 7414)
Advanced loss recovery algorithms (deferred to RFC 5681 and companions)
Detailed congestion control algorithms (referenced to RFC 5681, RFC 6298, RFC 2914)
High-performance extensions such as SACK, Window Scale, Timestamps (referenced to RFC 7323, RFC 2018)
Cryptographic security (delegated to IPsec, TLS, TCP-AO)

global_implementation_model.service_model: Reliable, in-order, connection-oriented, bidirectional byte-stream over unicast IP. Applications interact via a named-call API (OPEN, SEND, RECEIVE, CLOSE, ABORT, STATUS, FLUSH). Each direction is independently closeable (half-close). Port numbers multiplex flows per host-pair.
global_implementation_model.endpoint_model: Per-connection state held in a Transmission Control Block (TCB). A TCB is created on OPEN and deleted on CLOSE/ABORT/timeout. Each TCB holds local/remote socket pair, send/receive sequence variables (SND.UNA, SND.NXT, SND.WND, SND.UP, SND.WL1, SND.WL2, ISS, RCV.NXT, RCV.WND, RCV.UP, IRS), buffer pointers, retransmit queue pointer, security/compartment, and timer state.
global_implementation_model.state_model: Finite state machine with 11 named states: CLOSED (fictional, no TCB), LISTEN, SYN-SENT, SYN-RECEIVED, ESTABLISHED, FIN-WAIT-1, FIN-WAIT-2, CLOSE-WAIT, CLOSING, LAST-ACK, TIME-WAIT. Transitions are driven by three event categories: user calls, arriving segments, and timeouts.
global_implementation_model.message_model: Single message type: TCP segment. A segment consists of a fixed 20-byte header (extensible via options up to DOffset*4 bytes), an optional options region, and a variable-length data payload. Control is conveyed via eight named flag bits (CWR, ECE, URG, ACK, PSH, RST, SYN, FIN) and dedicated fields (SeqNo, AckNo, Window, UrgentPointer). Segment integrity is checked via a mandatory ones'-complement checksum over a pseudo-header + TCP header + data.
global_implementation_model.event_model: Three event categories drive TCB state transitions: (1) User Calls — OPEN, SEND, RECEIVE, CLOSE, ABORT, STATUS; (2) Arriving Segments — processed in a fixed eight-step pipeline per connection state (sequence check, RST, security, SYN, ACK, URG, data, FIN); (3) Timeouts — USER TIMEOUT, RETRANSMISSION TIMEOUT, TIME-WAIT TIMEOUT.
global_implementation_model.timer_model: Three timer classes per TCB: (1) Retransmission Timer (RTO) — dynamically computed per RFC 6298/Karn's algorithm, exponential backoff on expiry; (2) TIME-WAIT Timer — fixed 2×MSL (2×2 min = 4 min) after active close; (3) User Timeout — application-configurable per-connection send deadline. Additional implicit timers: Zero-Window Probe timer (first probe after one RTO, exponential backoff); Keep-Alive timer (default off, configurable ≥2 hr idle interval).
global_implementation_model.error_model: Error conditions propagate via: (1) synchronous error return strings on user calls (e.g., 'error: connection not open'); (2) asynchronous ERROR_REPORT upcall to application for soft TCP errors (ICMP receipt, excessive retransmissions, urgent pointer advance); (3) RST segment to remote peer; (4) TCB deletion and CLOSED state entry. Hard ICMP errors may abort the connection; soft ICMP errors MUST NOT.
runtime_dependencies: IP layer (IPv4 RFC 791 or IPv6 RFC 8200) — for routing, pseudo-header construction, TTL, Diffserv field, source address selection (GET_SRCADDR), MTU sizes (GET_MAXSIZES: MMS_S, MMS_R)
ICMP/ICMPv6 — for Path MTU Discovery signals and error delivery to TCP
Cryptographically secure PRF (e.g., hash of connection tuple + secret key) — for ISN generation
Clock providing ~4 µs resolution counter — for ISN clock component (M in ISN formula)
Congestion control module (RFC 5681) — slow start, congestion avoidance, fast retransmit/recovery
RTO computation module (RFC 6298) — RTT sampling with Karn's algorithm
Optional: Path MTU Discovery (RFC 1191/RFC 8201) or PLPMTUD (RFC 4821)
Optional: TCP Timestamps (RFC 7323) for PAWS and TIME-WAIT reduction

section_map.title: 3.8.5. The Communication of Urgent Information
section_map.primary_focus: Urgent mechanism: URG flag, urgent pointer semantics, urgent/normal mode transitions, asynchronous notification to application (MUST-32, MUST-33); new applications SHOULD NOT use (SHLD-13)

section_map.title: 3.8.6. Managing the Window
section_map.primary_focus: Window advertisement semantics; discouragement of window shrinking (SHLD-14); sender robustness against shrinking (MUST-34); introduction of ZWP

section_map.title: 3.8.6.1. Zero-Window Probing
section_map.primary_focus: ZWP mandatory (MUST-36): probe must be sent when window is zero; first probe after RTO, exponential backoff; receiver must ACK probes with zero window; connection must stay open as long as ACKs arrive (MUST-37)

merge_hints.reason: ZWP override timeout may share the SWS sender override timer

section_map.title: 3.8.6.2. Silly Window Syndrome Avoidance
section_map.primary_focus: SWS concept overview; Nagle vs sender SWS as complementary; defers to subsections for algorithms

section_map.title: 3.8.6.2.1. Sender's Algorithm -- When to Send Data
section_map.primary_focus: Sender SWS: usable window U = SND.UNA + SND.WND - SND.NXT; four send conditions; Max(SND.WND) as RCV.BUFF estimate; override timeout (0.1-1.0s); Fs=1/2 fraction

merge_hints.reason: Nagle and sender SWS are jointly applied to determine when to send

risk_notes: Four send conditions are ORed; the bracketed [SND.NXT = SND.UNA] part of conditions 2 and 3 is imposed by Nagle
Override timeout range 0.1-1.0s is implementation guidance only

section_map.title: 3.8.6.2.2. Receiver's Algorithm -- When to Send a Window Update
section_map.primary_focus: Receiver SWS: RCV.BUFF partitioned into RCV.USER + RCV.WND + Reduction; advance window only when reduction >= min(Fr*RCV.BUFF, Eff.snd.MSS); Fr=1/2

merge_hints.reason: delayed ACK and receiver SWS together determine when receiver sends an ACK with window update

risk_notes: Receiver uses its own Eff.snd.MSS assuming it equals sender's — this assumption must be noted in extracted algorithm IR

section_map.title: 3.8.6.3. Delayed Acknowledgments -- When to Send an ACK Segment
section_map.primary_focus: Delayed ACK: SHLD-18 recommendation; MUST-40 hard limit of <0.5s delay; SHLD-19 ACK every 2nd full-sized segment or 2*RMSS bytes

merge_hints.reason: receiver SWS and delayed ACK jointly determine receiver ACK timing

risk_notes: MUST-40: delay MUST be less than 0.5 seconds — this is a hard ceiling, not a guideline
GRO, ACK compression, ACK decimation are mentioned as current practice that further reduces ACK count — these are implementation notes, not normative requirements

defines.title: 3.10.7.1. CLOSED STATE
defines.primary_focus: Segment arrival in CLOSED: discard RST; for non-RST, generate RST with appropriate SEQ/ACK based on incoming ACK bit

merge_hints.reason: all segment arrival states form one contiguous procedure

defines.title: 3.10.7.2. LISTEN STATE
defines.primary_focus: Segment arrival in LISTEN: ignore RST; reject ACK with RST; on SYN with valid security, initialize TCB (RCV.NXT=SEG.SEQ+1, IRS=SEG.SEQ), choose ISS, send SYN-ACK, enter SYN-RECEIVED

merge_hints.reason: all segment arrival states form one contiguous procedure

risk_notes: Security compartment check on SYN is required before accepting in LISTEN state — must not be omitted

defines.title: 3.10.7.3. SYN-SENT STATE
defines.primary_focus: Segment arrival in SYN-SENT: five-step process (ACK check, RST check with RFC 5961 mitigation, security check, SYN check, drop). SYN+ACK → ESTABLISHED with ACK sent; SYN only → SYN-RECEIVED with SYN-ACK. SYN data handling note.

merge_hints.reason: SYN-SENT segment processing continues into 3.10.7.4 for data/FIN/URG after SYN

risk_notes: RFC 5961 mitigation: check SEQ exactly matches RCV.NXT before executing RST action — conditional on RFC 5961 implementation
Data in SYN segments is valid (T/TCP, TFO) — firewalls should not block; extractor should note this as implementation guidance

defines.title: 3.10.7.4. Other States
defines.primary_focus: Eight-step segment processing pipeline for all synchronized states (SYN-RECEIVED, ESTABLISHED, FIN-WAIT-1, FIN-WAIT-2, CLOSE-WAIT, CLOSING, LAST-ACK, TIME-WAIT): (1) sequence check, (2) RST, (3) security, (4) SYN, (5) ACK, (6) URG, (7) data, (8) FIN. RFC 5961 mitigations woven throughout. Window update logic (SND.WL1/SND.WL2). FIN processing state transitions.

merge_hints.reason: complete per-state transition details must merge with state machine overview from Figure 5
RST processing rules in step 2 refine 3.5.3 with RFC 5961 detail

risk_notes: This is the most dense section in the document; eight sequential checks must be applied in order and must not be reordered
RFC 5961 mitigation introduces conditional branching within steps 2 and 5; two code paths must be maintained
Window update rule: SND.WND updated only if SND.WL1 < SEG.SEQ OR (SND.WL1 = SEG.SEQ AND SND.WL2 <= SEG.ACK) — exact condition prevents stale window updates
MAX.SND.WND is a new variable introduced for RFC 5961 ACK check; not present in base RFC 793 TCB
FIN processing differs by current state — eight distinct outcomes must all be captured

defines.title: 3.10.8. Timeouts
defines.primary_focus: Three timeout handlers: USER TIMEOUT (flush, signal error, delete TCB, CLOSED), RETRANSMISSION TIMEOUT (resend front of retransmit queue, reset timer), TIME-WAIT TIMEOUT (delete TCB, CLOSED)

concepts.description: Fixed 20-byte header (extensible by options) containing: Source Port (16b), Destination Port (16b), Sequence Number (32b), Acknowledgment Number (32b), Data Offset (4b), Reserved (4b), Control Bits (8b: CWR ECE URG ACK PSH RST SYN FIN), Window (16b), Checksum (16b), Urgent Pointer (16b), Options (variable), Data (variable).

concepts.description: Three-group RST generation rules by connection state: (1) CLOSED: RST for all non-RST segments; (2) Non-synchronized (LISTEN/SYN-SENT/SYN-RECEIVED): RST on unacceptable ACK or security mismatch; (3) Synchronized: empty ACK for out-of-window; RST only for security mismatch. SEQ/ACK field selection rules differ per case.

related_concepts.evidence: RST sent by one peer triggers RST processing at the other

concepts.description: RST validation: SEQ must be in receive window (except SYN-SENT: SEG.ACK must match SYN). Per-state response: LISTEN ignores RST; SYN-RECEIVED (from LISTEN) returns to LISTEN; all others abort and go to CLOSED. RFC 5961 adds challenge-ACK variant for in-window but not-exact RST.

concepts.description: RFC 5961 mitigation: when RST or SYN is received in synchronized states with in-window but non-exact sequence number, send <SEQ=SND.NXT><ACK=RCV.NXT><CTL=ACK> as a challenge instead of immediately resetting. Throttling of challenge ACKs is implementation-specific.

related_concepts.evidence: Challenge ACK is an alternative to direct RST action specified in RFC 5961

concepts.description: Three-case close: (1) local-initiated: ESTABLISHED→FIN-WAIT-1 on CLOSE; (2) remote-initiated: ESTABLISHED→CLOSE-WAIT on FIN receipt, then LAST-ACK on local CLOSE; (3) simultaneous: both→CLOSING→TIME-WAIT. concepts.description: IPv4 ICMP Dest Unreachable codes 2-4. SHOULD abort the connection (SHLD-26). Some implementations do not abort in synchronized states.
concepts.implementation_role: Hard error handling: connection normally aborted

concepts.description: For all synchronized states (SYN-RECEIVED through TIME-WAIT): (1) sequence number check; (2) RST bit check [RFC 5961 variant]; (3) security/compartment check; (4) SYN bit check [RFC 5961 challenge ACK]; (5) ACK field check [RFC 5961 MAX.SND.WND]; (6) URG bit check; (7) segment text delivery; (8) FIN bit check. Steps must execute in order.
concepts.implementation_role: Core segment handling logic; authoritative per-state behavior for all synchronized states

related_concepts.evidence: Step 1 applies acceptability test
Steps 2 and 4 may use challenge ACK per RFC 5961

concepts.description: All sequence number, ACK, and window arithmetic is modulo 2^32. The symbol '=<' in this document means 'less than or equal (modulo 2^32)'. Sequence space wraps from 2^32-1 to 0.
concepts.implementation_role: Fundamental arithmetic model for all sequence number operations; must use unsigned 32-bit wraparound comparison

concepts.description: 6-bit DSCP value in IP header. Application MUST be able to specify per connection (MUST-48); specified independently per direction. TCP SHOULD pass unchanged to IP (SHLD-22). Generally SHOULD NOT change during connection (SHLD-23).
concepts.implementation_role: QoS marking for IP layer; set by application, passed through by TCP

do_not_over_extract_from.reason: Appendix A.1 is non-normative discussion of obsolete TOS→Diffserv transition

concepts.description: Kind=2, Length=4, MSS value (2 bytes). Communicates maximum receive segment size. MUST be sent/received (MUST-14). MUST NOT be sent on non-SYN segments (MUST-65). Defaults: 536 (IPv4) or 1220 (IPv6) if not received (MUST-15).
concepts.implementation_role: Negotiates receive buffer limit at connection setup

related_concepts.evidence: SendMSS in Eff.snd.MSS formula is the received MSS option value

concepts.description: Kind=0, no length field. Signals end of option list; may precede end of header according to DOffset.
concepts.implementation_role: Option list terminator; required only when option list doesn't extend to header boundary

concepts.description: Kind=1, no length field. Used as padding between options to align subsequent options on word boundaries.
concepts.implementation_role: Option padding; receiver must handle options at any byte boundary (MUST-64)

concepts.description: 1-bit control flag (bit offset 14 in 16-bit aligned view). Indicates synchronization of sequence numbers; used at connection initiation. Occupies one sequence number.
concepts.implementation_role: Connection initiation signal; triggers ISN exchange in 3WHS

related_concepts.evidence: SYN consumes one sequence number

concepts.description: 1-bit control flag (bit offset 11). When set, the Acknowledgment Number field is valid. Once ESTABLISHED, always sent.
concepts.implementation_role: Enables cumulative acknowledgment mechanism

related_concepts.evidence: ACK flag makes acknowledgment number field meaningful

concepts.description: 1-bit control flag (bit offset 13). Instructs receiver to delete connection without further interaction. Receipt of RST must never generate another RST.
concepts.implementation_role: Abortive connection reset signal

related_concepts.evidence: Incoming RST triggers reset processing procedure

concepts.description: 1-bit control flag (bit offset 15). Signals sender will send no more data or control occupying sequence space. Occupies one sequence number. Implies PUSH.
concepts.implementation_role: Graceful close signal; triggers close state transitions

related_concepts.evidence: FIN occupies one sequence number

concepts.description: 1-bit control flag (bit offset 10). When set, the Urgent Pointer field is meaningful.
concepts.implementation_role: Activates urgent data mechanism

related_concepts.evidence: URG flag makes urgent pointer field meaningful

concepts.description: 1-bit control flag (bit offset 12). Signals that data should be pushed through to receiving application. Not a record marker; implementation may coalesce successive PSH bits.
concepts.implementation_role: Hints to receiver to deliver data promptly; set on last buffered segment when PUSH flag not supported

concepts.description: 1-bit control flag (bit offset 8). Congestion Window Reduced; defined in RFC 3168 for ECN.
concepts.implementation_role: ECN congestion notification signal

related_concepts.evidence: CWR and ECE are the ECN flag pair (RFC 3168)

concepts.description: 1-bit control flag (bit offset 9). ECN-Echo; defined in RFC 3168 for ECN.
concepts.implementation_role: ECN echo signal from receiver to sender

concepts.description: Retransmission count or time threshold at which negative advice is sent to IP layer (dead gateway diagnosis) and application is notified. SHOULD correspond to at least 3 retransmissions (SHLD-10).
concepts.implementation_role: Triggers network-layer diagnosis and application warning

related_concepts.evidence: Reaching R1 triggers ERROR_REPORT to application

concepts.description: Retransmission count or time threshold at which connection is closed. SHOULD correspond to at least 100 seconds (SHLD-11). Application MUST be able to set R2 (MUST-21). For SYN: MUST be ≥3 minutes (MUST-23).
concepts.implementation_role: Connection failure threshold; triggers connection abort

related_concepts.evidence: Exceeding R2 causes connection close

concepts.description: Application MUST specify source route on active OPEN (MUST-51); it MUST override datagram source route (MUST-52). On passive OPEN with completed IP Source Route, TCP MUST save and use return route (MUST-53); later source routes SHOULD override (SHLD-24).
concepts.implementation_role: Ensures TCP checksum validity and return path correctness with IP source routing

concepts.description: SYN with invalid source address MUST be ignored (MUST-63). SYN addressed to broadcast or multicast MUST be silently discarded (MUST-57).
concepts.implementation_role: Prevents spurious connection state and replay attacks

concepts.description: TCP endpoint responds to three event categories: (1) User Calls: OPEN, SEND, RECEIVE, CLOSE, ABORT, STATUS; (2) Arriving Segments: SEGMENT ARRIVES; (3) Timeouts: USER TIMEOUT, RETRANSMISSION TIMEOUT, TIME-WAIT TIMEOUT. Model is immediate-return with delayed signal.
concepts.implementation_role: Architectural model for all TCP state machine behavior

related_concepts.evidence: Event processing drives FSM state transitions

concepts.description: Per-connection user-settable timeout. On expiry from any state: flush all queues, signal 'error: connection aborted due to user timeout', delete TCB, enter CLOSED. Default global 5 minutes.
concepts.implementation_role: Application-level send deadline; triggers abort if data not delivered in time

concepts.description: On TIME-WAIT TIMEOUT expiry: delete TCB, enter CLOSED state. Restarted if retransmitted remote FIN arrives during TIME-WAIT.
concepts.implementation_role: Enforces 2×MSL linger; triggers final TCB cleanup

related_concepts.evidence: Timer duration = 2×MSL = 4 minutes

concepts.description: SND.WND is updated only if (SND.WL1 < SEG.SEQ) OR (SND.WL1 = SEG.SEQ AND SND.WL2 <= SEG.ACK). Updates SND.WL1←SEG.SEQ, SND.WL2←SEG.ACK alongside SND.WND←SEG.WND.
concepts.implementation_role: Prevents out-of-order ACKs from incorrectly shrinking the send window

related_concepts.evidence: Rule governs when SND.WND is updated from incoming SEG.WND
SND.WL1 is the sequence number guard variable

concepts.description: RFC 5961 variable: largest window ever received from peer (subject to window scaling), or a hard-coded maximum permissible window value. Used in RFC 5961 ACK validation check: SEG.ACK must satisfy (SND.UNA - MAX.SND.WND) <= SEG.ACK <= SND.NXT.
concepts.implementation_role: RFC 5961 guard against blind data injection; not in original RFC 793 TCB

related_concepts.evidence: MAX.SND.WND used in step 5 ACK validation per RFC 5961

concepts.description: RFC 2018/2883 option; recommended but not required for basic TCP interoperability.
concepts.implementation_role: High-performance loss recovery; defined externally

do_not_over_extract_from.reason: Only listed as a reference; not defined in this document

concepts.description: RFC 7323 option; recommended for high-performance. Used for PAWS (Protection Against Wrapped Sequences) and TIME-WAIT reduction (SHLD-4).
concepts.implementation_role: PAWS and TIME-WAIT optimization; defined externally

do_not_over_extract_from.reason: Only referenced; not defined in this document

related_concepts.evidence: Timestamps enable TIME-WAIT reduction per RFC 6191

concepts.description: RTT sampling algorithm: do not use retransmitted segments for RTT measurement. Defined in RFC 6298. Required by MUST-18.
concepts.implementation_role: Prevents spurious RTO updates from retransmitted segments

do_not_over_extract_from.reason: Only referenced via MUST-18; algorithm defined in RFC 6298

concepts.description: After reboot or loss of sequence number memory, host SHOULD delay emitting TCP segments for one MSL to prevent stale segment confusion. Not necessary in most modern implementations due to ISN randomization and faster reboots.
concepts.implementation_role: Historical reliability measure for post-reboot ISN uniqueness; largely obsolete

related_concepts.evidence: Quiet time duration = one MSL (2 minutes)

concepts.description: One peer has closed/rebooted while the other believes the connection is still ESTABLISHED. Detected when the still-connected peer receives a RST in response to unexpected data or SYN.
concepts.implementation_role: Anomaly condition handled by RST exchange; auto-resolves on data attempt

related_concepts.evidence: RST is used to signal and resolve half-open connections

co_extract_groups.topic: TCP Segment Header Format and Option Definitions
co_extract_groups.reason: Header field definitions (3.1), option formats (3.2), and bit-offset IANA registry (Section 6) together constitute the complete wire format. Figure 1 (TCP header bitmap), Figure 2 (IPv4 pseudo-header), Table 7 (flag offsets), and option diagrams all require visual extraction.

The FSM overview (3.3.2/Figure 5) and all event processing sections (3.10.1-3.10.8) together define the complete state machine. Figure 5 must be visually extracted; event processing sections provide the authoritative per-state action tables. All must be co-extracted to produce a complete FSM IR.

TCB variables (3.3.1), sequence number mechanics (3.4), ISN generation (3.4.1), and quiet time (3.4.2-3.4.3) together define the complete sequence number subsystem. Figures 3 and 4 (sequence space diagrams) must be visually extracted.

3WHS mechanics (3.5), half-open handling (3.5.1), RST generation (3.5.2), RST processing (3.5.3), OPEN call processing (3.10.1), LISTEN segment arrival (3.10.7.2), and SYN-SENT processing (3.10.7.3) are one interconnected establishment mechanism. Figures 6-11 (sequence diagrams) require visual extraction.

Close sequence (3.6), half-closed/TIME-WAIT rules (3.6.1), CLOSE call event processing (3.10.4), and ABORT call event processing (3.10.5) define the teardown mechanism. Figures 12-13 require visual extraction.

Window management (3.8.6), ZWP (3.8.6.1), SWS overview (3.8.6.2), sender SWS algorithm (3.8.6.2.1), receiver SWS algorithm (3.8.6.2.2), and delayed ACK (3.8.6.3) together define the complete flow-control subsystem.

Segmentation overview (3.7), MSS option (3.7.1), PMTUD (3.7.2), variable MTU (3.7.3), Nagle algorithm (3.7.4), and Jumbograms (3.7.5) define the segmentation subsystem; all interact through Eff.snd.MSS.
RTO requirement (3.8.1), congestion control hooks (3.8.2), connection failure thresholds R1/R2 (3.8.3), and timeout handlers (3.10.8) form the reliability timer subsystem.
Urgent mechanism definition (3.8.5) and URG bit processing (step 6 of 3.10.7.4) together define the complete urgent data implementation.
All API call format definitions (3.9.1.x) define the user-facing TCP API contract; they should be extracted together to capture the complete interface specification.
TCP/lower-level interface (3.9.2), source routing (3.9.2.1), ICMP handling (3.9.2.2), and source address validation (3.9.2.3) define the TCP↔IP integration boundary.

RST generation rules (3.5.2), RST processing rules (3.5.3), CLOSED state RST behavior (3.10.7.1), and synchronized state RST check in 3.10.7.4 step 2 must all be co-extracted to produce complete RST handling IR.

split_warnings.reason: Section 3.10.7.4 covers eight sequential processing steps across eight connection states (SYN-RECEIVED through TIME-WAIT) — this is the largest and most complex single section. Extractors should process each of the eight steps as a separate sub-procedure IR, then union them by state. Do not attempt single-pass extraction.

Section 3.4.1 contains both the ISN selection algorithm and the three-way handshake description; these are distinct mechanisms that should be extracted into separate IR families (algorithm IR for ISN, procedure IR for 3WHS).
Appendix B spans 10 pages (88-97) as a single multi-page requirements table; it should be processed as a series of visual table extraction requests rather than as a single text extraction unit.

reference_only_nodes.reason: Status of This Memo — IETF boilerplate, no protocol content
Copyright Notice — legal boilerplate, no protocol content
3.2.1 only lists SACK/Timestamps/Window Scale by name; all definitions are in external RFCs
3.2.2 only points to RFC 4727 and RFC 6994; no normative content here
Section 5 (Changes from RFC 793) is a changelog; useful for diff-tracing but not for protocol IR extraction
Section 7 (Security) is informative; does confirm which mechanisms are in-spec but does not define new procedures
Appendix A.1 (IP Security Compartment and Precedence) — non-normative implementation note; MLS-specific
Appendix A.1.1 (Precedence) — non-normative; describes obsolete TOS→Diffserv transition
Appendix A.1.2 (MLS Systems) — non-normative; MLS-specific IPSO/CIPSO/CALIPSO notes
Appendix A.2 (Sequence Number Validation) — non-normative; describes edge cases for simultaneous open/self-connects
Appendix A.3 (Nagle Modification) — non-normative; describes Minshall modification not in standard
Appendix A.4 (Low Watermark Settings) — non-normative; SO_SNDLOWAT/SO_RCVLOWAT/TCP_NOTSENT_LOWAT socket options

extraction_depth_rules.rule: overview sections may introduce concepts but should not produce full procedure/message IR unless the behavior is actually defined there
message format IR should be extracted from sections that define fields/layout/options, not from sections that merely mention message names or flags
procedure IR should be extracted from sections that define ordered behavior, not from sections that only list API names or events
state machine IR may be extracted from overview diagrams, but visual_requests should be emitted when topology depends on a figure/table

Figure 5 (TCP Connection State Diagram) is explicitly labeled 'only a summary and must not be taken as the total specification' — FSM IR must always be supplemented from section 3.10 event processing, not built from Figure 5 alone
all sequence number comparisons use modulo-2^32 unsigned arithmetic; the symbol '=<' in this document means 'less than or equal modulo 2^32' and must not be treated as standard comparison

RFC 5961 mitigations (challenge ACK for RST and SYN in synchronized states; MAX.SND.WND guard for ACK validation) introduce conditional code paths in section 3.10.7 that must be represented as separate branches in procedure IR, not folded into the base RFC 793 logic

requirement labels (MUST-N, SHLD-N, MAY-N, REC-N) appear throughout the document; each represents a distinct normative requirement that should be preserved as an annotation on extracted IR, not discarded
Appendix A nodes are explicitly non-normative; do not generate MUST-level procedure IR from any Appendix A section

external RFCs referenced for algorithms (RFC 5681 for congestion control, RFC 6298 for RTO computation, RFC 7323 for timestamps/window scale, RFC 2018/2883 for SACK) must be flagged as runtime_dependencies in extracted IR, not reproduced as inline algorithms

open_questions.question: The FIN-WAIT-1→TIME-WAIT shortcut transition (when FIN is received and local FIN is simultaneously acknowledged) is explicitly noted as omitted from Figure 5. Is this transition covered completely in 3.10.7.4 step 8, or is there additional prose elsewhere?
open_questions.why_it_matters: Missing this transition would produce an incomplete FSM IR that fails to handle the simultaneous FIN+ACK case correctly

open_questions.question: Section 3.10.7.4 step 5 introduces MAX.SND.WND as a new TCB variable for RFC 5961 support, but this variable is not listed in the TCB definition in section 3.3.1. Should extractors add MAX.SND.WND to the TCB variable list or treat it as a local per-check variable?
open_questions.why_it_matters: If MAX.SND.WND must persist across segments it is a TCB field; if it can be computed on the fly from the window history it may not need TCB storage

open_questions.question: The SWS override timer (0.1-1.0 seconds) described in section 3.8.6.2.1 is noted as potentially shareable with the ZWP timer (section 3.8.6.1). Is this a normative option or merely an implementation suggestion?
open_questions.why_it_matters: If the timers can be unified, the timer model needs only two timers instead of three in the flow-control subsystem

open_questions.question: The 3WHS description in section 3.4.1 states that the ISN formula is SHLD-1 (SHOULD) while the clock-driven component is MUST-8 (MUST). How should extractors handle an implementation that uses a clock-driven ISN without a PRF component — is it partially conformant?
open_questions.why_it_matters: The distinction between MUST-8 and SHLD-1 affects whether a conformance checker can pass an implementation lacking the PRF

open_questions.question: Section 3.10.7.3 (SYN-SENT) notes that data may be included with SYN segments (TCP Fast Open, T/TCP). The acceptance and processing of this data is described informally. Is there a normative path for delivering SYN data to the application before ESTABLISHED is reached?
open_questions.why_it_matters: TFO data delivery timing affects buffering and application interface semantics

open_questions.question: Section 3.9.1.2 states that when PUSH flags are not implemented, PSH bit must be set on the 'last buffered segment' (MUST-61). The document does not define exactly when buffering ends — is 'last buffered' determined by current send buffer or send window?
open_questions.why_it_matters: Ambiguity in MUST-61 could lead to non-interoperable PSH bit behavior

open_questions.question: Section 5 lists 19 incorporated errata. Are any of these errata changes behavioral (affecting procedure IR) rather than purely textual, and if so, which sections in section 3 reflect which errata?
open_questions.why_it_matters: If an errata changed a specific procedure, extractors should know which version of the text is authoritative
